title: Soil cadmium and pyrene drive earthworm burrowing inhibition and modify mucus characteristics: Toward non-destructive biomarkers.
author_string: Wang, Weipeng, Wang, XiaoSa, Su, Run, Xu, Qian, Wang, Congying et al.
abstract: Earthworms have been widely recognized as sensitive bioindicators for soil pollution monitoring, yet the impacts of contaminants on their behaviorally-mediated ecological processes remain poorly understood. Our study investigated the effects of environmentally relevant concentrations of cadmium (Cd) and pyrene (Pye) on earthworm avoidance and burrowing behaviors, with parallel characterization of characteristics (chemical properties, FTIR profiles, metabolome). In dual-chamber tests, earthworms exhibited significant avoidance only at 100 mg kg⁻¹ Pye, while Cd elicited no avoidance at field-relevant levels. Conversely, in 2D - terraria incubation assays, burrowing activity showed dose-dependent inhibition for both contaminants, suggesting burrowing parameters may serve as more sensitive pollution indicators than avoidance responses. Comprehensive mucus analyses revealed significant contaminant-driven alterations: chemical assays detected changes in TOC, NH₄⁺-N, NO₃⁻-N, and Olsen-P correlated with exposure levels; FTIR spectra demonstrated modified molecular compositions, including emerging fingerprint-region peaks; metabolomics identified 20 conserved differential metabolites (predominantly organic acids and nucleotides) across treatments. Notably, we established novel correlations between behavioral changes and mucus compositional shifts, suggesting secretory biochemistry may mediate ecological behaviors under pollution stress. These findings preliminarily validate non-destructive biomarkers (burrowing parameters and mucus signatures), elucidate conserved metabolic responses, and reveal behavior-secretion coupling under pollution stress-advancing soil ecotoxicology and contaminated land management.
